metadata.subject: English Language Arts
metadata.description: New Media 12 is the capstone digital media course, developing students as sophisticated media analysts, creators, and ethical communicators. Students engage with advanced questions of media power, platform governance, AI-generated content, and digital equity. They produce a substantial portfolio of original digital work demonstrating mastery across multiple media forms. The course prepares students for post-secondary study and careers in media, communications, journalism, and digital arts, while developing the critical literacy needed to navigate an increasingly complex digital world.
metadata.general_description: The capstone digital media course developing advanced media analysis, portfolio-level production, and critical digital citizenship — preparing students for post-secondary media, communications, and digital careers.

big_ideas.statement: New media systems have profound and uneven effects on democracy, culture, and equity.
big_ideas.elaboration: Students examine the systemic dimensions of digital media — platform monopolies, algorithmic discrimination, digital divides, and the concentration of media power — and their implications for democracy and justice.

big_ideas.statement: Masterful digital communicators blend technical skill, artistic vision, and ethical responsibility.
big_ideas.elaboration: Graduating students synthesize technical production competency, creative vision, and ethical awareness into a coherent identity as media professionals and citizens.

big_ideas.statement: The future of media is shaped by the choices of media producers and citizens.
big_ideas.elaboration: Students understand that digital media systems are not inevitable — they reflect human choices and can be reshaped by informed, active citizens and media makers committed to equity and truth.

big_ideas.statement: Indigenous and marginalized communities are reclaiming digital media as a tool for self-representation and sovereignty.
big_ideas.elaboration: Students engage with how First Peoples and marginalized communities are using digital tools to assert their own narratives, preserve cultures, and build political power.

competencies.raw: Critically analyze complex digital media ecosystems for power, equity, and democratic implications; evaluate advanced questions of AI-generated content, platform governance, and digital rights; synthesize diverse perspectives on the role of media in society.
i_can_statements: I can conduct sophisticated analysis of digital media ecosystems, examining platform governance, algorithmic power, and media monopolies.
I can evaluate emerging issues — AI content generation, deepfakes, digital rights, and data colonialism — using ethical and critical frameworks.
I can synthesize diverse scholarly, journalistic, and community perspectives on the social role of digital media.
I can connect Indigenous digital sovereignty to global questions of media equity and self-determination.

competencies.raw: Produce a substantial portfolio of original digital work demonstrating mastery across multiple media forms; demonstrate professional-level technical skill, creative vision, and ethical responsibility; present and defend creative work with articulate critical reflection.
i_can_statements: I can produce a substantial, polished portfolio of original digital work across multiple media forms.
I can demonstrate professional-level technical skill in at least two digital production domains.
I can articulate and defend my creative vision and the ethical choices embedded in my media work.
I can contribute meaningfully to digital communities and public conversations as a responsible media maker.
I can reflect critically on my development as a digital communicator and set goals for post-graduation growth.

areas.name: Advanced Media Analysis
areas.raw: Platform power, AI media, digital equity, and media systems analysis.
topics: Platform governance: how technology companies make decisions about content moderation and user rights
AI-generated content: deepfakes, synthetic media, large language models, and implications for truth
Digital monopolies: concentration of media power and its effects on democracy and culture
Algorithmic justice: bias in AI systems and the movement for algorithmic accountability
Digital colonialism: data extraction, platform imperialism, and Indigenous data sovereignty
Media futures: emerging technologies and their potential impacts on communication and society

areas.name: Advanced Video and Cinematic Production
areas.raw: Advanced video production, documentary filmmaking, and cinematic storytelling.
topics: Advanced cinematic technique: camera movement, colour grading, and visual language
Long-form documentary production: research, ethics, structure, and post-production
Video essay and personal essay film: blending argument, narrative, and cinematic craft
Collaborative film production: crew roles, communication, and professional workflow
Festival and distribution: submitting work to film festivals and online platforms
Developing a distinctive visual voice and directorial perspective

areas.name: Journalism and Digital Publishing
areas.raw: Digital journalism, investigative reporting, and ethical publishing.
topics: Investigative journalism: research methods, source verification, FOIA requests, and data journalism
News writing for digital platforms: inverted pyramid, SEO, and multimedia integration
Editorial ethics: fairness, accuracy, conflict of interest, and editorial independence
Multimedia journalism: combining text, audio, video, and interactive elements
Community journalism: serving local and underrepresented communities through digital media
Indigenous and community media: models of media ownership and editorial sovereignty

areas.name: Digital Portfolio and Professional Development
areas.raw: Portfolio development, professional digital presence, and media career pathways.
topics: Portfolio curation: selecting, sequencing, and presenting a body of digital work
Artist and creator statement: articulating creative vision, values, and aspirations
Professional digital presence: personal website, social media, and networking for media careers
Post-secondary pathways: film, journalism, communications, digital arts, and computer science programs
Freelance and independent media: platforms, licensing, and sustainable creative careers
Media industry landscape: understanding the contemporary media job market

areas.name: Media, Democracy, and Social Change
areas.raw: Digital activism, civic media, and the role of media in social movements.
topics: Digital activism: how social movements use media to organize, amplify, and achieve change
Counter-narratives: challenging dominant media representations through independent digital production
Media for truth and reconciliation: Indigenous-led media and decolonizing digital storytelling
Global digital rights: freedom of expression, press freedom, and internet governance
Media entrepreneurship and alternative models: co-ops, non-profits, and community-owned media
Developing a personal media ethics manifesto and civic commitment statement
